steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researchers studied Serbian customers from a developing economy and found they prioritize speedy delivery and tracking over package aesthetics. They then "cautioned against applying the findings to customers generally." The blank must complete the researchers' own explanation of why that caution is warranted — i.e., what limitation of the study prevents broad generalization.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — online retailers globally should prioritize delivery speed and tracking: This applies the findings broadly, which is exactly what the researchers cautioned against. ✗
  • Choice B — additional research with participants from countries of varying economic development is needed: This identifies the actual limitation — the sample came from one country with one economic profile — and calls for research that would address it. Fits the researchers' caution precisely. ✓
  • Choice C — the findings may not hold with a larger group of Serbian participants: The limitation is geographic and economic scope, not Serbian sample size. More Serbian respondents would not address the problem of generalizing to other countries. ✗
  • Choice D — customers in wealthier countries likely value package design more: This speculates about a specific outcome without calling for further research, and the passage gives no basis for this claim. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
